description: Final dissertation benchmark suite. Prompt text and fixture token counts are fixed for final evidence runs.

dataset_metadata.approval_state: content approved; ready for final evidence
dataset_metadata.notes: Dataset derived from CNN/DailyMail. Fixed offline.

final_short_01.text: Summarize this event: City officials opened three cooling centers after a week of record heat strained clinics, while emergency crews reported fewer overnight calls once the centers began offering water, charging stations, pharmacy referrals, and rides for older residents.

final_short_02.text: Summarize this event: A coastal rail service resumed limited operations after engineers repaired a storm-damaged signal bridge, and commuters were told that full weekday schedules would return once two safety inspections and a backup power test are completed.

final_short_03.text: Summarize this event: Hospital leaders launched a weekend vaccination drive at five community schools, saying the clinics would prioritize children, shift workers, families who missed earlier appointments, and residents needing help with transport delays or registration forms.

final_short_04.text: Summarize this event: A regional court ordered the release of flood recovery funds after auditors confirmed that temporary housing contracts met public bidding rules and could begin within two weeks under weekly public reporting, safety checks, and tenant hotlines.

final_short_05.text: Summarize this event: Teachers and parents agreed to extend after-school tutoring through the summer after district data showed reading scores improving fastest at campuses that paired small groups with regular attendance calls, library access, and meal support.

final_medium_01.text:
Officials in Riverton said the latest review of the river transit upgrade began after residents asked for clearer evidence about costs, safety, and public access. Maya Chen, who leads the local planning office, told reporters that the work would be measured against published milestones rather than campaign promises. The project team described a cautious schedule, with early inspections, public briefings, and independent checks before any permanent changes are approved. Community groups welcomed the slower pace because previous announcements had left families uncertain about whether services would move, expand, or close. Robert Hale, a researcher at the regional policy institute, said the strongest argument for the proposal was not speed but the chance to reduce repeated emergency spending. The article notes that several agencies have already shared raw budget tables, but it also says those tables are difficult for residents to compare without a plain explanation. Supporters argue that the plan could make routine services easier to reach, especially for older residents and workers who cannot attend daytime hearings. Critics respond that the same plan could shift risk onto smaller neighborhoods if oversight boards approve construction before staffing and maintenance plans are complete. A temporary advisory panel will meet twice a month, publish minutes, and invite written comments from families, business owners, and transport operators. The city council is expected to vote only after the panel releases a final summary that separates confirmed findings from assumptions that still need field testing. The article notes that several agencies have already shared raw budget tables, but it also says those tables are difficult for residents to compare without a plain explanation. Supporters argue that the plan could make routine services easier to reach, especially for older residents and workers who cannot attend daytime hearings.

Summarize the main arguments and identify two key individuals mentioned in the article above.

final_long_01.text:
The first article in this compilation follows Mesa County officials as they reopen the public record on the regional water resilience program after months of conflicting estimates. Amira Patel, the program director, said the review would preserve every raw inspection note, contract revision, and meeting transcript so outside readers could trace the conclusion. The report begins with a timeline of early warnings, including delayed maintenance, confusing public notices, and a staffing plan that assumed vacant posts would be filled quickly. Residents interviewed for the article said they did not oppose investment, but they wanted a reliable way to compare promised benefits with the disruption caused by construction. Jon Ellis, an independent auditor, described the central invariant as simple: no recommendation should be accepted unless its evidence can be tied back to a named source and a dated record. A second article examines risk controls, focusing on procurement limits, emergency exceptions, and the problem of approving work before monitoring equipment has been calibrated. The writers explain that short deadlines can create a false sense of certainty because officials may report activity instead of outcomes that residents can actually observe. Several engineers argue that the safest plan is to stage the work, test each phase, publish failures, and avoid changing the evaluation criteria after early results appear. Carla Gomez, who represents a neighborhood coalition, said the outcome would be judged by whether families see fewer service interruptions rather than by the size of the headline budget. The compilation also includes a sidebar on communication failures, noting that residents often received technical summaries without context, maps without dates, and schedules without owners. Another section compares three possible outcomes: full approval with safeguards, limited approval for the least risky pieces, or a delay until missing evidence is supplied. The authors conclude that the strongest plan is the one that keeps records public, preserves independent review, and treats uncertainty as a reason for staged testing rather than silence. They warn that the weakest plan would combine broad promises with narrow logs, making it impossible to distinguish genuine progress from a convenient narrative after the fact. The final paragraphs return to the practical issue of trust, arguing that transparency works only when readers can inspect the same facts that decision makers used. final_long_02.text:
The first article in this compilation follows Easthaven officials as they reopen the public record on the urban housing repair backlog after months of conflicting estimates. Hannah Brooks, the program director, said the review would preserve every raw inspection note, contract revision, and meeting transcript so outside readers could trace the conclusion. The report begins with a timeline of early warnings, including delayed maintenance, confusing public notices, and a staffing plan that assumed vacant posts would be filled quickly. Residents interviewed for the article said they did not oppose investment, but they wanted a reliable way to compare promised benefits with the disruption caused by construction. Owen Miller, an independent auditor, described the central invariant as simple: no recommendation should be accepted unless its evidence can be tied back to a named source and a dated record. A second article examines risk controls, focusing on procurement limits, emergency exceptions, and the problem of approving work before monitoring equipment has been calibrated. The writers explain that short deadlines can create a false sense of certainty because officials may report activity instead of outcomes that residents can actually observe. Several engineers argue that the safest plan is to stage the work, test each phase, publish failures, and avoid changing the evaluation criteria after early results appear. Lena Ortiz, who represents a neighborhood coalition, said the outcome would be judged by whether families see fewer service interruptions rather than by the size of the headline budget. The compilation also includes a sidebar on communication failures, noting that residents often received technical summaries without context, maps without dates, and schedules without owners. Another section compares three possible outcomes: full approval with safeguards, limited approval for the least risky pieces, or a delay until missing evidence is supplied. The authors conclude that the strongest plan is the one that keeps records public, preserves independent review, and treats uncertainty as a reason for staged testing rather than silence. They warn that the weakest plan would combine broad promises with narrow logs, making it impossible to distinguish genuine progress from a convenient narrative after the fact. The final paragraphs return to the practical issue of trust, arguing that transparency works only when readers can inspect the same facts that decision makers used. final_long_03.text:
The first article in this compilation follows Clearfield officials as they reopen the public record on the emergency broadband rollout after months of conflicting estimates. Samuel Wright, the program director, said the review would preserve every raw inspection note, contract revision, and meeting transcript so outside readers could trace the conclusion. The report begins with a timeline of early warnings, including delayed maintenance, confusing public notices, and a staffing plan that assumed vacant posts would be filled quickly. Residents interviewed for the article said they did not oppose investment, but they wanted a reliable way to compare promised benefits with the disruption caused by construction. Iris Novak, an independent auditor, described the central invariant as simple: no recommendation should be accepted unless its evidence can be tied back to a named source and a dated record. A second article examines risk controls, focusing on procurement limits, emergency exceptions, and the problem of approving work before monitoring equipment has been calibrated. The writers explain that short deadlines can create a false sense of certainty because officials may report activity instead of outcomes that residents can actually observe. Several engineers argue that the safest plan is to stage the work, test each phase, publish failures, and avoid changing the evaluation criteria after early results appear. Marta Silva, who represents a neighborhood coalition, said the outcome would be judged by whether families see fewer service interruptions rather than by the size of the headline budget. The compilation also includes a sidebar on communication failures, noting that residents often received technical summaries without context, maps without dates, and schedules without owners. Another section compares three possible outcomes: full approval with safeguards, limited approval for the least risky pieces, or a delay until missing evidence is supplied. The authors conclude that the strongest plan is the one that keeps records public, preserves independent review, and treats uncertainty as a reason for staged testing rather than silence. They warn that the weakest plan would combine broad promises with narrow logs, making it impossible to distinguish genuine progress from a convenient narrative after the fact. The final paragraphs return to the practical issue of trust, arguing that transparency works only when readers can inspect the same facts that decision makers used. final_long_04.text:
The first article in this compilation follows Bay District officials as they reopen the public record on the coastal hospital consolidation plan after months of conflicting estimates. June Park, the program director, said the review would preserve every raw inspection note, contract revision, and meeting transcript so outside readers could trace the conclusion. The report begins with a timeline of early warnings, including delayed maintenance, confusing public notices, and a staffing plan that assumed vacant posts would be filled quickly. Residents interviewed for the article said they did not oppose investment, but they wanted a reliable way to compare promised benefits with the disruption caused by construction. Victor Lane, an independent auditor, described the central invariant as simple: no recommendation should be accepted unless its evidence can be tied back to a named source and a dated record. A second article examines risk controls, focusing on procurement limits, emergency exceptions, and the problem of approving work before monitoring equipment has been calibrated. The writers explain that short deadlines can create a false sense of certainty because officials may report activity instead of outcomes that residents can actually observe. Several engineers argue that the safest plan is to stage the work, test each phase, publish failures, and avoid changing the evaluation criteria after early results appear. Nadia Flores, who represents a neighborhood coalition, said the outcome would be judged by whether families see fewer service interruptions rather than by the size of the headline budget. The compilation also includes a sidebar on communication failures, noting that residents often received technical summaries without context, maps without dates, and schedules without owners. Another section compares three possible outcomes: full approval with safeguards, limited approval for the least risky pieces, or a delay until missing evidence is supplied. The authors conclude that the strongest plan is the one that keeps records public, preserves independent review, and treats uncertainty as a reason for staged testing rather than silence. They warn that the weakest plan would combine broad promises with narrow logs, making it impossible to distinguish genuine progress from a convenient narrative after the fact. The final paragraphs return to the practical issue of trust, arguing that transparency works only when readers can inspect the same facts that decision makers used. final_soak_01.text: The following is a news excerpt. Regional health officials said a new mobile clinic program will start with three neighborhoods in May, expand to rural stops by July, and publish weekly appointment data so residents can see whether evening hours reduce missed visits. The program is funded for one year, with renewal tied to wait times, patient surveys, and the number of follow-up appointments completed within two weeks. Nurses will also coordinate with local food banks, school offices, and senior centers to advertise routes before each monthly schedule is finalized. Summarize the key benefits and timeline mentioned in this excerpt.
